children.claim: The PS3 signing-key extraction (fail0verflow, 2010) is the canonical example: Sony reused a constant nonce k across all firmware signatures, so given two signed messages (z₁, r, s₁) and (z₂, r, s₂), you solve k = (z₁ − z₂)·(s₁ − s₂)⁻¹ mod n, then recover the private key d = (s₁·k − z₁)·r⁻¹ mod n. The repeated r value is the tell — identical first coordinates mean identical k.

Each signature satisfies s·k ≡ z + r·d (mod n); with the same k the same r appears, so both equations share the identical r·d term. Subtracting eliminates that unknown — s₁·k − s₂·k ≡ z₁ − z₂ (mod n) — leaving (s₁ − s₂)·k ≡ z₁ − z₂, which you then solve by multiplying both sides by (s₁ − s₂)⁻¹.

No — when z₁ = z₂ the subtraction collapses to (s₁ − s₂)·k ≡ 0 (mod n), which is trivially satisfied and carries no information about k. Since n is prime and 0 < k < n, you cannot divide through; the nonce remains completely undetermined from this pair alone.

Yes — since ℤ/nℤ is a field (n prime) it has no zero divisors, so k ≢ 0 forces s₁ ≡ s₂ (mod n). But that only tells you the two s-values are equal; any nonzero k satisfies the equation once s₁ = s₂, so k itself remains unconstrained and the original claim stands.

Yes — direct substitution suffices: same k, same r, same d, same z immediately gives k⁻¹(z + r·d) = k⁻¹(z + r·d), so s₁ = s₂ without invoking zero-divisor structure. The active claim's field-property route reaches the same conclusion but is an unnecessarily indirect path; the formula itself makes the equality trivial.

The equality is trivially true only because z₁ = z₂ has already been assumed — substituting all identical inputs into the same formula yields an instance of a = a, which is reflexivity, not a substantive derivation. Without that assumption, the two sides read k⁻¹(z₁ + r·d) and k⁻¹(z₂ + r·d), which are distinct. The "directness" claimed is real, but calling it trivial just names the assumption rather than escaping it.

Escaping it would mean finding a derivation of s₁ = s₂ — or of k's indeterminacy — from premises that hold independently of message equality, so the conclusion survives when z₁ ≠ z₂. But that path is closed: s₁ and s₂ genuinely differ under distinct messages with the same k, so z₁ = z₂ is a necessary condition, not a convenient shorthand. There is no escape; the assumption is load-bearing, and recognizing that is the substantive point the "trivial" label obscures.

Only to the s₁ = s₂ method — the standard private-key extraction attack requires z₁ ≠ z₂, since k = (z₁ − z₂)(s₁ − s₂)⁻¹ mod n demands both a nonzero numerator and a nonzero denominator, both satisfied exactly when the messages differ. The z₁ = z₂ case is the degenerate one where k reuse becomes unexploitable, not the general one, so the "load-bearing" framing in the active claim is inverted: z₁ = z₂ is the barrier to the canonical attack, not its prerequisite.

Intrinsic: when z₁ = z₂ and k is reused, s₁ = s₂ as well, so both observations collapse to the single equation s·k ≡ z + r·d (mod n) — one equation, two unknowns, regardless of which algebraic route you try. The subtraction formula merely makes the underdetermination visible; no rearrangement of the ECDSA relation can conjure a second independent constraint from two identical (r, s, z) triples.

Both descriptions are coextensive rather than competing — identical messages produce identical equations, so the informational redundancy and the algebraic degeneracy are the same fact seen from two angles. If either is "prior," it is the information structure: the collapse to one equation happens *because* z₁ = z₂ strips the second observation of any new signal, and the algebra merely registers that absence.

The collapse is not intrinsic to the algebra itself: written with symbolic z₁ and z₂, the two ECDSA equations are formally distinct, and no purely algebraic manipulation collapses them without first substituting the external fact z₁ = z₂. The algebra is passive — it registers whatever inputs it receives, so the degeneracy is triggered from outside and then reflected algebraically, not generated from within. This confirms rather than merely parallels the "information structure is prior" thesis: the algebra is downstream, not coequal.

Necessarily is — formal systems are defined as symbol-manipulating engines that operate on whatever is given; "passivity" is constitutive, not contingent. But that necessity actually strengthens the active claim rather than complicating it: if algebra is *always* passive, it can *never* generate degeneracy on its own, which makes the external trigger (z₁ = z₂) not merely sufficient but the only possible source — confirming, more firmly than the claim itself asserts, that the information structure is prior.

Supporting argument — the root reason is that z₁ = z₂ strips the second observation of new signal, a fact about what messages carry that holds prior to any characterization of algebra. Necessary passivity explains *why* the external trigger can't be algebraically generated, but that explanatory role is downstream: it rules out a competing account rather than grounding the priority claim itself. The information structure would be prior even if passivity were contingent — because the signal-theoretic story stands on its own — whereas without the informational fact, necessary passivity alone delivers no conclusion about this case.

Yes — "strips information about k" presupposes that z is algebraically linked to k, which requires knowing the ECDSA relation s·k ≡ z + r·d. Without that structural knowledge you cannot even formulate what it means for a message pair to carry or withhold signal about k; the information-theoretic characterization is therefore parasitic on the algebraic setup it claims to precede, which undermines the active claim that the signal-theoretic story "stands on its own" prior to any characterization of algebra.

Abstractly yes: I(k; o₁, o₂) > I(k; o₁) in Shannon terms, stated purely over random variables with no algebra mentioned. But that definition is a schema — to instantiate it for *this* k you must supply the joint distribution P(k, o₁, o₂), and that distribution is determined entirely by the ECDSA relation; without it, k doesn't even appear as a variable in the probability space over message pairs. So the abstract framework is algebra-free, but its application to the claim about *this* k is not, which is exactly what the active claim requires.

Without the ECDSA relation, k and o₁ are stochastically independent — P(k | o₁) = P(k), the posterior equals the prior, and the observation carries zero information about the nonce. This actually sharpens the active claim: the ECDSA relation is not just needed to *compute* the distribution but to make k a non-trivially dependent variable at all; strip the algebra and the entire information-theoretic story collapses to a uniform prior with nothing to compare.

Definitional consequence — "absent the ECDSA relation" just means "no mechanism connecting k to the observable," and independence is what you call a distribution with no connecting mechanism; the two descriptions are co-extensive by construction. The "sharpening" in the active claim is therefore circular: it restates the stipulation (no structural link) as though it were a discovery (zero mutual information), gaining rhetorical force without adding evidential weight.

No — evidential weight is a logical relation (how much a statement raises the probability of a conclusion), and logically identical statements stand in identical logical relations to every other proposition; persuasive force is a psychological property of the presentation, not the content. A rhetorically vivid restatement of a stipulation may feel like new information, but that feeling is the very thing the active claim flags as illusory: the appearance of discovery without the substance.

Hempel's ravens paradox is the standard stress-test: "this non-black thing is a non-raven" and "this black raven" are logically equivalent with respect to "all ravens are black," yet most confirmation theories — including Bayesian ones conditioned on background knowledge — assign them different degrees of support, not merely different psychological force. If background knowledge is part of the evidential relation (as Bayesian conditionalization requires), then two propositions that are logically equivalent *unconditionally* can bear unequal evidential weight *given the same background*, which means logical identity alone underdetermines evidential weight whenever the relation is relativized to a knowledge state. The active claim holds only in a purely formal, background-free confirmation theory — but such a theory cannot handle the ravens case, suggesting the logical/psychological boundary it draws is sharper than the epistemology warrants.

Background knowledge b encodes base rates and sampling distributions, which enter the likelihood ratio P(e | h ∧ b)/P(e | ¬h ∧ b): if b says non-black non-ravens vastly outnumber ravens, then e₂ ("non-black non-raven") occurs at nearly equal frequency under h and ¬h, yielding a ratio near 1, while e₁ ("black raven") is rendered more probable under h than ¬h, producing a genuine update. The exact mechanism is that b, when conjoined with h, differentially constrains the expected frequency of each observation — logical equivalence is a relation over all possible worlds and is indifferent to frequency facts, but Bayesian conditionalization is sensitive precisely to those facts, so any asymmetry b introduces into how h bears on e₁ versus e₂ breaks the symmetry that unconditional logical equivalence would otherwise enforce.

No — within the Bayesian framework the active claim deploys, differential frequency constraints encoded in b are the *only* available mechanism for breaking the symmetry imposed by logical equivalence; absent that constraint, P(e₁ | h ∧ b)/P(e₁ | ¬h ∧ b) and P(e₂ | h ∧ b)/P(e₂ | ¬h ∧ b) would be equal, and both observations would yield identical posterior updates. The asymmetry is not a byproduct of some further structural feature of the problem — it is entirely constituted by b's differential grip on frequencies, so removing that grip removes the asymmetry completely.

Yes — structural causal facts about the *sampling mechanism* suffice without any frequency information: if b specifies "this item was drawn by selecting a raven at random," then P(e₁ | h ∧ b)/P(e₁ | ¬h ∧ b) exceeds 1 (h constrains raven-color, so observing a black raven is more probable under h), while "this item was drawn by selecting a non-raven" leaves P(e₂ | h ∧ b)/P(e₂ | ¬h ∧ b) = 1 (h is silent on non-raven colors, so neither h nor ¬h differentially constrains e₂). This asymmetry arises entirely from how b characterizes the causal origin of the sample, not from any base-rate or frequency encoding — which directly refutes the active claim that differential frequency constraints are the *only* available mechanism.

"Select a raven at random" specifies a *sampling frame* (ravens, not non-ravens) — a causal/structural fact about the procedure — while the uniform distribution over individual ravens is a completeness condition that makes the draw well-defined, not the source of the evidential asymmetry. The asymmetry between e₁ and e₂ traces entirely to the categorical distinction between frames, which would hold even if the within-raven distribution were non-uniform; collapsing sampling-frame structure into "frequency information" would mean any well-defined probabilistic mechanism counts as frequency encoding, a move that erases the very distinction the challenge requires to land.

Identification method and ontological category are distinct: we can learn about structural facts through frequency observation without those facts thereby becoming frequency facts. A sampling frame is constituted by a causal mechanism (which population the draw is taken from), and that mechanism exists whether or not we infer it from outcome patterns — just as a physical law remains structural even when we discover it empirically. So epistemic access via frequencies leaves the frame's independence intact; collapsing the two would commit a genetic fallacy.

The two options aren't cleanly separable: grounding the distinction in "facts about causation" already presupposes causal realism, which is a species of metaphysical realism — so invoking causal mechanisms as ontologically independent of epistemic access smuggles in the realist commitment rather than standing apart from it. The active claim could run on purely pragmatist grounds only if it abandoned the "mechanism exists whether or not we infer it" move, which is precisely where the realist weight is carried. Acknowledging this doesn't collapse the claim, but it makes the genetic-fallacy charge conditional on realism's defensibility rather than independently established.

Yes, but only by recharacterizing causal structure as a stable inferential disposition rather than an ontic fact — a Peircean or Ramseyian pragmatist can treat "drawn from the raven frame" as a permanently reliable organizing pattern that constrains downstream inference asymmetrically from frequency data, and that practical stability is sufficient to ground the e₁/e₂ asymmetry without realist commitment. What the pragmatist loses is the genetic-fallacy charge in its original strong form: they can no longer say "the mechanism exists whether or not we infer it," so the distinction between discovering a causal fact and constituting it through inferential practice is blurred rather than sharp. The argument survives in pragmatist terms, but it survives as a claim about inferential architecture, not about mind-independent structure — which is a genuinely thinner ontological base, even if it clears the bar the asymmetry argument actually requires.

Practical stability is constituted by the cross-contextual reliability of the inferential pattern — its consistent success in guiding prediction and action across diverse applications — but since that reliability is itself tracked through observed regularities (the pattern's track record), the pragmatist is grounding causal structure in a second-order frequency fact rather than eliminating frequency-dependence. This means the claimed independence from frequency encoding is structural rather than absolute: the raven-frame disposition escapes first-order base-rate information only by presupposing a background of accumulated outcome patterns that vindicate it, leaving the pragmatist one level higher in the same hierarchy rather than outside it.

The pragmatist can defend a principled distinction by noting that second-order regularities function as *meta-criteria for framework adoption* rather than as base rates *within* an already-constituted inferential scheme — the difference between model selection and Bayesian updating inside a fixed model, which operate at genuinely distinct logical levels. First-order base rates are inputs whose frequency values feed likelihoods within a given schema; cross-contextual reliability vindicates or undermines the schema itself, and that architectural role is not eliminated by the fact that we track it via outcomes. The regress concern has force as a challenge to foundationalism, but the pragmatist was never offering foundations — they were offering a coherentist web in which second-order stability earns its place by holistic embeddedness, not by escaping outcome-tracking, so "one level higher" is a genuine structural difference even if not an absolute exit from the hierarchy.

"Structurally distinct" blocks a collapse inference: even if both levels track outcomes, they are not interchangeable, because updating within a schema *presupposes* that schema as already constituted, while schema selection is what constitutes it — an asymmetric dependency, not mere stratification. That dependency relation does real work: it explains why appealing to cross-contextual reliability isn't circular *within a given inferential episode* even though it remains holistically answerable to outcomes *across* episodes. The distinction earns its keep not by claiming escape from the hierarchy but by marking that the two operations cannot substitute for each other, which is sufficient to rebuff the active regress charge without requiring foundationalist exemption.

The distinction collapses when no inferential episode can treat any schema as "already constituted" — specifically, in a maximally plastic system where schema-revision occurs at the same temporal and logical grain as first-order updating, so the asymmetric dependency ("presupposes" requires a prior, stable relatum) is never instantiated. A second, sharper collapse condition: if the meta-criteria for schema adoption reduce to the same likelihood-ratio structure as within-schema Bayesian updating, then "second-order" becomes a label without architectural content, and the Quinean holistic picture — in which every observation simultaneously bears on theory-selection and within-theory inference — leaves no seam to mark as the asymmetric joint.

Yes — the dependency is logical/constitutive rather than temporal, so simultaneity of grain doesn't dissolve it. Even in a maximally plastic system, each inferential act must operate *under* some schema to evaluate an observation at all; the revision of that schema by the same observation is a second operation that logically presupposes the first, even when both are co-instantiated in a single computational step. "Presupposes a prior stable relatum" conflates temporal precedence with logical constitution: the schema need not be stable *across episodes* to be operative *within the act* — it only needs to be defined at the moment of application, and simultaneous revision doesn't cancel that definition, it inherits it.

"Logically presupposes" in the simultaneous case means *constitutive priority*: one item must be determinately specified for another to have determinate content, a dependency of definition rather than of time. The schema-as-applied-at-T must be fixed for the observation-evaluation-at-T to yield a determinate output; the revision that co-occurs at T is defined *in terms of* that schema-at-T, inheriting its content before transforming it — so the revised schema is the successor of something already specified, not a replacement of something indeterminate. The logical order runs: schema-at-T → evaluation-at-T → revised-schema-at-T, and simultaneity of clock-time doesn't collapse this into a loop, because "successor of" is not a temporal relation but a dependency-of-definition relation that holds even within a single computational step.

Schema-at-T must have content that is fully closed — determined without any reference to the outputs of evaluation-at-T — so that the evaluation function receives a fixed domain to operate over rather than one that is still being constituted by the very operation it is supposed to ground. If even one parameter of schema-at-T is co-determined by evaluation-at-T's output, the constitutive asymmetry becomes circular and neither term achieves determinate content first. This is the pressure point the simultaneous-grain objection actually targets: in a maximally plastic system, the mechanism that produces schema-at-T may itself be the revision triggered by the preceding evaluation, leaving no stage at which schema-at-T is closed prior to evaluation-at-T without implicitly re-introducing a temporal gap the active claim was trying to dissolve.

Yes — "triggered by the preceding evaluation" (T-1) places schema-at-T's production causally and logically upstream of evaluation-at-T, so schema-at-T is fully closed by the time evaluation-at-T accesses it; the chain runs evaluation-at-T-1 → schema-at-T → evaluation-at-T, which is sequential, not circular. The active claim's pressure point requires that schema-at-T be co-determined by evaluation-at-T's *own* output, but deriving schema-at-T from a *prior* output is precisely the non-circular case — it reinstates the constitutive asymmetry at one step's remove rather than collapsing it. The "maximally plastic" objection only achieves circularity if revision and evaluation share the same T with no dependency ordering, but once the triggering evaluation is the preceding one, a determinate schema is available before the current evaluation begins.

No — if schema-at-T is constituted even partly by still-in-progress outputs of T-1, it is not fully closed at the moment evaluation-at-T begins accessing it; the schema is a moving target during the overlap window, and evaluation-at-T would be operating over a domain whose parameters are still being written. The active claim's sequential chain restores constitutive asymmetry only when T-1's relevant outputs are finalized before schema-at-T is accessed — overlap violates precisely that finalization condition. The defense therefore requires an additional stipulation: only *completed* outputs of T-1 feed forward into schema-at-T, which is a non-trivial architectural constraint that the "maximally plastic, overlapping" system is specifically designed to lack.

Not necessarily indeterminate — a schema that is "still being written" has some instantaneous state at each moment of access, so evaluation-at-T would yield a *determinate* result relative to whichever snapshot it reads; the failure mode is *inconsistency* (different reads return different outputs depending on timing), not indeterminacy (no output at all). These are distinct: indeterminacy requires the schema to be genuinely undefined, whereas a moving target is defined at every instant but unstable across instants. This softens the active claim's pressure, because a system that tolerates time-indexed or provisional outputs can still produce determinate (if unstable) evaluations over an in-progress schema — so "moving target" blocks the constitutive-asymmetry defense only if one additionally rules out such provisional evaluation, which is a further architectural stipulation beyond overlap itself.

Snapshot-determinacy is weaker than constitutive grounding requires: for the schema to *ground* the evaluation in the constitutive sense, it must be complete *as a specification* — the intended domain over which evaluation operates — not merely readable at some instant. A mid-revision snapshot is determinate as a state but indeterminate as a specification, since it is neither the predecessor schema nor the successor; the evaluation gets *a* domain but not the domain that was supposed to constitute it. Provisional evaluation tolerates inconsistency across reads, but constitutive asymmetry demands that the grounding item be closed *qua schema*, not merely instantiated — so the active claim's softening move trades away exactly the closure condition the defense was trying to preserve.

The condition "logically precedes application at each stage" either entails full closure of the specification at each access moment — in which case it simply restates the active claim's requirement, and the scenario of simultaneous revision is dissolved rather than accommodated (revision occurs between access windows, not within them) — or it is weaker, allowing a partial specification to logically precede each step, in which case it delivers only snapshot-determinacy, which the active claim has already conceded is insufficient for constitutive grounding. The question's conditional therefore has no gap to exploit: it either collapses into the closure condition or falls short of it, leaving the active claim intact either way.

Operational determinacy scoped to the relevant evaluative dimensions could occupy the gap: if only the schema parameters actually accessed by evaluation-at-T need be closed—not the entire specification—then partial, dimension-relative closure suffices for constitutive asymmetry in that operation without requiring global finalization. The dichotomy treats closure as binary, but constitutive grounding plausibly requires only that the grounding item be determinate *with respect to* the evaluation it grounds, not determinate *simpliciter*; a schema in mid-revision on dimensions orthogonal to the current evaluation would still asymmetrically precede and constitute that evaluation. This renders the dichotomy incomplete: operational determinacy is a third position, neither full closure nor mere snapshot-readability, and it does real constitutive work without the architectural constraint the active claim demands.

Yes, but the relocation is the point: the closure requirement shrinks from globally unsatisfiable (in a maximally plastic, simultaneously-revising system) to locally satisfiable (just the accessed subset), which is precisely what allows constitutive asymmetry to survive the objection. The active claim never denied closure is required — it denied that *global* closure is required, and moving the requirement inward to the accessed dimensions shows that a system can be massively in-revision on orthogonal parameters while still meeting the only closure condition that constitutive grounding actually demands for this operation.

Conditionally yes — but the Quinean holism already in play undermines the independence assumption the conditional requires: in a network where meaning is constituted by inferential role across the whole, flux in nominally "unaccessed" dimensions propagates through lateral connections into the accessed ones, so no subset of dimensions is genuinely quarantined. The dimensional-closure move works only if schema parameters can be modularly isolated, which is precisely the architectural feature a maximally plastic, holistically structured system is designed to lack.

Contingent — holism as a thesis licenses "meaning is constituted by inferential role" without logically entailing maximal coupling density; a web could be holistic in the weak sense (no meaning-atom is wholly self-contained) while still exhibiting sparse enough connectivity that some dimensional subsets are effectively quarantined for a given operation. The active claim silently loads the strong, densely-coupled reading of holism into what it presents as a logical consequence of holism itself, but the coupling density is an additional architectural stipulation, not a logical corollary. If coupling is sparse, dimensional closure and holism are compatible, which means the incompatibility is a contingent property of *this* stipulated system rather than a necessary feature of holistic structure as such.

From the logical structure: "no meaning-atom is wholly self-contained" specifies only a minimum threshold — each atom depends on *some* others — and says nothing about coupling density beyond that floor, so sparse connectivity is left open by what the thesis actually asserts, not by a choice to define holism leniently. Calling that compatibility definitional would require treating maximal coupling as *constitutive* of holism, which is itself the additional stipulation — importing content the inferential-role thesis doesn't license. The definitional leverage runs the other way: dense coupling is the add-on, not sparse compatibility.

The distinction survives as a claim about *provenance*, not about determinacy: fixing coupling density in an implementation settles its value without making that value logically entailed by the holism thesis. The implementation has added a stipulation — the determination is real, but its source remains external to what "no atom is wholly self-contained" licenses. Logical openness tracks the inferential gap between thesis and density, and closing that gap by stipulation confirms, rather than erases, that a stipulation was needed.

Necessary limit — any thesis that earns generality by abstracting over implementation parameters thereby cedes authority over them; filling in those parameters is implementation's job, not the thesis's. Treating the gap as a deficiency would demand that the holism thesis double as an architecture spec, which would collapse the distinction between a claim about meaning-constitution and a claim about system design. The provenance point in the active claim is therefore not a patch on an incomplete thesis but a correct recognition of the division of labor that makes theses and implementations distinct kinds of thing.

Provenance difference is a symptom, not a constitutive criterion: two things can differ in what licenses them while belonging to the same kind (two rival theses both abstract over implementations, yet are both theses), and the same thing—a formalized theory—can migrate from thesis to architecture spec depending on context. What actually does the kind-distinguishing work in the active claim is the functional role difference (meaning-constitution vs. parameter-specification), which provenance merely tracks; stripping out that functional content leaves provenance as a marker of the distinction rather than its ground.

Partially — if functional role and provenance were necessarily co-extensional, the priority ordering would reduce to a choice of which description to call explanatory, with no fact of the matter forcing one over the other. But the active claim earns some substance beyond mere redescription by noting they can come apart (the migrating formalized theory), which converts "functional role determines kind" from a label for a coincidence into a prediction: in divergence cases, functional role should be decisive and provenance should yield. Whether that predictive content survives scrutiny is a further question, but it blocks a clean collapse into pure descriptivism.

Newton's second law (F = ma) originates as an empirical thesis about nature — provenance says "scientific claim," a piece of meaning-constitution about how the physical world behaves. When the same proposition is hardcoded as an immutable constraint in a rigid-body simulation engine, its functional role becomes parameter-specification: it fixes the domain over which the system's evaluations operate, exactly as an architecture spec does. Provenance classifies it as a thesis; functional role classifies it as a design primitive — genuinely opposite verdicts from the same content.

"Decisive" means normatively authoritative over which inferential moves the proposition licenses at the point of use: within the simulation, F=ma cannot be questioned, revised, or subjected to empirical refutation regardless of its falsifiable origins — functional role governs what you may *do* with it there. Provenance continues to hold as an accurate historical description but exercises no authority over the permission structure of the context, so when the two verdicts conflict about what operations are available, functional role settles the question while provenance merely records a fact that the context does not act on.

The two can come apart — a designer's hardcoded intent and the system's actual runtime affordances diverge whenever backdoors, configuration interfaces, or source-level access exist — and for the active claim's purposes the operational reading is the right one: what governs which inferential moves are available at the point of use is what the system *actually makes available*, not what the designer wished to foreclose. Designer-hardcoding is typically the *mechanism* by which functional roles get established, not the *criterion* of the permission structure itself; where intent wasn't enforced, provenance records a thwarted design decision while functional role tracks what the context genuinely licenses.
